<meta name="citation_abstract" content="Researchers worldwide are increasingly interested in methods for reproducing organogenesis by culturing spheroids and organoids. Unlike spheroids, organoids are more closely related to organs in structure and functional activity. Due to the diversity of organoid production protocols and the lack of precise morphological criteria for standardizing these methods, there is a growing need to identify morphological features as significant distinguishing indicators of mature and functionally healthy organoids. Various semi- and automated programs have been developed to address these issues. This review examines programs for the morphometric analysis of spheroids and organoids. Most of these programs utilize images taken with a brightfield microscope and a confocal laser microscope.">

Salmina  <sup>1,2</sup><!----><!----></p></a></li><!--]--><!----><li class="last-people"><div class="PeopleListItem" href=""><div class="Avatar Avatar--size-40 Avatar--text Avatar--grey PeopleListItem__Avatar"><!--[--><span class="notranslate">S</span><span class="notranslate">N</span><!--]--></div><p class="PeopleListItem__name notranslate">Sergey N. Illarioshkin  <sup>1</sup><!----><!----></p></div></li><!----><li class="PeopleList__spacer" aria-hidden="true"></li></ul><div class="AffiliationList"><ul class="AffiliationList__list"><!--[--><li class="AffiliationList__item"><p class="AffiliationList__name notranslate"><span>1.</span> Brain Science Institute, Russian Center of Neurology and Neurosciences, Moscow, Russia</p></li><li class="AffiliationList__item"><p class="AffiliationList__name notranslate"><span>2.</span> Bauman Moscow State Technical University, Moscow, Russia</p></li><!--]--><!----></ul></div></div><div style="display:none;" class="ArticleMetrics"><p class="ArticleMetrics__title"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 48 48" fill="currentColor" width="16px" style="vertical-align:middle;display:inline-block;height:auto;"><path d="M39.798 40.512a1.227 1.227 0 0 0 1.2-1.233v-1.434a1.22 1.22 0 0 0-1.2-1.224H37.02l.024-24.402a1.2 1.2 0 0 0-1.2-1.2h-6.93a1.2 1.2 0 0 0-1.2 1.2v24.45h-3.732V21.54a1.2 1.2 0 0 0-1.2-1.2h-6.98a1.2 1.2 0 0 0-1.2 1.2v15.117h-3.778V8.7a1.2 1.2 0 0 0-1.2-1.2H8.19a1.2 1.2 0 0 0-1.2 1.2v30.615a1.2 1.2 0 0 0 1.2 1.2l31.608-.003Zm-21.444-3.9V24.105h1.89V36.6l-1.89.012Zm13.098 0V14.82h1.866v21.792h-1.866Z"></path></svg> Article metrics </p><a class="ArticleMetrics__link" href="#metrics" data-event="articleMetrics-a-viewDetails">View details</a><!----></div><!----><div class="ArticleDetailsV4__main__content"><div class="ArticleContent"><!--[--><!--]--><div id="h1"><h2>Abstract</h2><!--[--><p>Researchers worldwide are increasingly interested in methods for reproducing organogenesis by culturing spheroids and organoids. Unlike spheroids, organoids are more closely related to organs in structure and functional activity. Due to the diversity of organoid production protocols and the lack of precise morphological criteria for standardizing these methods, there is a growing need to identify morphological features as significant distinguishing indicators of mature and functionally healthy organoids. Various semi- and automated programs have been developed to address these issues. This review examines programs for the morphometric analysis of spheroids and organoids. Most of these programs utilize images taken with a brightfield microscope and a confocal laser microscope.</p><!--]--></div><!----><!--[--><div id="s1"><!--[--><h2>1 Introduction</h2><p>In recent years, there has been a growing number of publications on the production and application of three-dimensional multicellular <!--[--><i>in vitro</i><!--]--> models—spheroids and organoids. A distinctive feature of spheroids is that they consist of immortalized cell lines or primary culture cells, while organoids are formed from embryonic stem cells (ESCs), induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), or somatic stem cells from healthy and diseased patients (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B8-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b8"><!--[--><span id="B8a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Boot et al., 2024<!--]--></button>; <button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B101-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b101"><span id="B101a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span>Zhao et al., 2022</button>; <button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B44-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b44"><!--[--><span id="B44a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Lee et al., 2017<!--]--></button>). Organoids more accurately reproduce the physiological composition of the microenvironment of the corresponding tissues <!--[--><i>in vivo</i><!--]--> than spheroids, but the process of organoid formation is more complex and time-consuming. Therefore, spheroids are increasingly used for standard biophysical studies and high-throughput drug screening (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B8-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b8"><!--[--><span id="B8a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Boot et al., 2024<!--]--></button>), while organoids, as revolutionary three-dimensional (3D) cell models, have become an indispensable tool in fundamental research in developmental biology, as well as in disease modeling, due to their ability to self-organize and mimic the structure and functions of real organs (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B48-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b48"><!--[--><span id="B48a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Lukonin et al., 2020<!--]--></button>). Thus, organoids reproduce organ-like structures—miniature versions of organs grown <!--[--><i>in vitro</i><!--]-->. To date, such mini-organs as intestinal organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B14-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b14"><!--[--><span id="B14a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Cieza et al., 2021<!--]--></button>), kidney organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B29-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b29"><!--[--><span id="B29a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Huang et al., 2020<!--]--></button>), tumor organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B30-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b30"><!--[--><span id="B30a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Huo et al., 2023<!--]--></button>), breast organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B11-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b11"><!--[--><span id="B11a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Cairns et al., 2020<!--]--></button>), lung organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B62-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b62"><!--[--><span id="B62a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Pauli et al., 2017<!--]--></button>), and cerebral organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B42-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b42"><!--[--><span id="B42a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Lancaster et al., 2013<!--]--></button>) have already been obtained. Cerebral organoids derived from iPSCs have particular research and practical value, as they provide the ability to reproduce specific features of human brain tissue that cannot be studied <!--[--><i>in vivo</i><!--]--> in animal models. Due to their physiological proximity, cerebral organoids derived from iPSCs represent more relevant models of various neurological and neurodegenerative diseases. For example, post-mortem histological brain samples represent the terminal stage of the disease and therefore do not allow us to trace the mechanisms of neurological disease development at the cellular level. Furthermore, ethical restrictions hinder brain tissue biopsy (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B59-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b59"><!--[--><span id="B59a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Nam et al., 2020<!--]--></button>). Eichmüller and Knoblich note that animal models, used in many studies, also cannot provide a comprehensive source of information on disease pathogenesis, as the mechanisms of development in humans and animals are not always identical (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B20-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b20"><!--[--><span id="B20a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Eichmüller and Knoblich, 2022<!--]--></button>). In contrast, iPSC-derived organoids are complex, patient-specific models consisting, like tissues, of different cell types or cells at different stages of maturity, allowing to reproduce <!--[--><i>in vitro</i><!--]--> the pathogenetic processes characteristic of humans.</p><p>The field of organoids research is advancing at an unprecedented pace. The transition from subjective visual assessment to objective quantitative analysis is fundamental to improving the reproducibility, scalability, and statistical significance of research. The constant emergence of new imaging technologies and software tools necessitates the systematization and critical evaluation of existing knowledge. The existing diversity of microscopy methods and software solutions for morphometric analysis, each with its own unique advantages and disadvantages, requires in-depth analysis. Such analysis will enable researchers to make informed decisions when selecting the most appropriate tools for specific tasks. This review is also necessary to identify current gaps, unresolved issues, and limitations in the field of organoid morphometric analysis, which can serve as a starting point for future innovative research and technological developments.</p><p>The review included publications describing visualization methods directly or potentially applicable to the analysis of three-dimensional (3D) and four-dimensional (4D) cellular models. Particular attention was given to articles containing specific quantitative data on the performance of the methods (e.g., segmentation accuracy, processing speed) and a detailed description of the software functionality. Both specialized tools developed specifically for organoid analysis and more general bioimage analysis platforms that can be successfully adapted for organoid morphometry were considered.</p><p>Obtaining high-quality images is only the first step in the morphometric analysis of organoids. Extracting quantitative data requires automated or semi-automated software that can segment objects (highlight their boundaries), measure various morphological parameters, and analyze their dynamics. The development of 3D and 4D cell models has led to an exponential increase in data complexity and the demands on visualization and analysis methods. This, in turn, has created a fundamental challenge for morphometric methods, as simple 2D measurements have become insufficient to characterize complex three-dimensional structures and their changes over time. Thus, there is a need to develop new, more powerful microscopic approaches and computational algorithms capable of processing volumetric and temporal data.</p><div class="ArticleContent_pnr"><!--[--><p>The purpose of this literature review is to examine protocols for obtaining spheroids and organoids, to investigate methods for visualizing 3D models, and to analyze modern software tools for morphometric analysis, identifying their key characteristics, advantages, disadvantages, and areas of application. We examined these aspects for various types of spheroids and organoids, focusing on our research subjects of immediate interest—cerebral organoids. To achieve this goal, the following objectives were set:</p><ul list-type="order"><!--[--><li><!--[--><p>To describe methods for obtaining and visualizing spheroids and organoids, as well as methods for analyzing the resulting images (specialized software solutions and platforms for segmentation and quantitative morphometric analysis of organoid images).</p><!--]--></li><!--]--><!--[--><li><!--[--><p>To conduct a comprehensive comparative analysis of the reviewed methods and tools, identifying their strengths and weaknesses, as well as specific application areas.</p><!--]--></li><!--]--></ul><!--]--></div><!--]--></div><div id="s2"><!--[--><h2>2 Characteristics of organoids and assembloids production methods</h2><p>The earliest publication (2001) on the differentiation of neurons from human pluripotent stem cells was an article on the formation of neural rosettes from embryoid bodies, and the first publication (2013) on the production of cerebral organoids from human pluripotent stem cells was the work of a group of scientists led by <button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B42-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b42"><!--[--><span id="B42a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Lancaster et al. (2013)<!--]--></button>. Lancaster et al. stimulated the differentiation of embryoid bodies in the neuroectodermal direction in Matrigel using a rotating bioreactor. Moreover, Y.J. Hong et al. developed a protocol for the production of cerebral organoids from human induced pluripotent cells and from human embryonic stem cells (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B28-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b28"><!--[--><span id="B28a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Hong et al., 2022<!--]--></button>). Thus, a variety of protocols have been developed for the creation of 3D structures from iPSCs obtained from patient skin fibroblasts or peripheral blood mononuclear cells. Organoid production protocols vary in terms of medium composition and technological aspects, and the organoids themselves differ in the representation of different cell types (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B8-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b8"><!--[--><span id="B8a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Boot et al., 2024<!--]--></button>). Over the past decades, methods have been developed for producing cerebral organoids capable of reproducing specific brain regions: the hippocampus (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B66-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b66"><!--[--><span id="B66a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Pomeshchik et al., 2020<!--]--></button>), cerebellum (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B5-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b5"><!--[--><span id="B5a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Ballabio et al., 2020<!--]--></button>), midbrain (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B32-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b32"><!--[--><span id="B32a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Jo et al., 2016<!--]--></button>), pituitary gland (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B35-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b35"><!--[--><span id="B35a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Kano et al., 2023<!--]--></button>), thalamus (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B23-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b23"><!--[--><span id="B23a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Fligor et al., 2021<!--]--></button>), and cerebral cortex (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B21-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b21"><!--[--><span id="B21a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Eiraku et al., 2008<!--]--></button>; <button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B42-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b42"><!--[--><span id="B42a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Lancaster et al., 2013<!--]--></button>; <button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B61-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b61"><!--[--><span id="B61a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Paşca et al., 2015<!--]--></button>).</p><p>One of the most common production methods, suspension culture, is being modified and improved. Standardization of embryonic body differentiation was achieved using low-adhesion U-shaped plates, microarrays, and the hanging drop method. A bioreactor and orbital shaker were used during the agitation stage of cerebral organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B81-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b81"><!--[--><span id="B81a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Shnaider, 2018<!--]--></button>). In 2017, Lancaster et al. used bioengineered PLGA (poly(lactic-co-glycolic acid)) microfibrils to create a floating scaffold mimicking the cortex’s basement membrane. This approach promoted improved neuroectodermal differentiation and proper formation of cortical tissue cytoarchitecture compared to the standard protocol (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B43-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b43"><!--[--><span id="B43a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Lancaster et al., 2017<!--]--></button>).</p><p>Laboratory-produced three-dimensional brain organoids have become alternatives to classical animal and <!--[--><i>in vitro</i><!--]--> models. Cerebral organoids have become a convenient tool for patient-specific modeling of hereditary and sporadic forms of neurological, neurodegenerative and neuropsychiatric diseases, including Alzheimer’s disease (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B98-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b98"><span id="B98a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span>Yan et al., 2018</button>), Parkinson’s disease (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B37-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b37"><!--[--><span id="B37a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Kim et al., 2019<!--]--></button>), microcephaly caused by Zika virus infection (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B40-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b40"><!--[--><span id="B40a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Krenn et al., 2021<!--]--></button>), schizophrenia (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B99-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b99"><span id="B99a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span>Ye et al., 2017</button>), autism spectrum disorders (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B50-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b50"><!--[--><span id="B50a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Mariani et al., 2015<!--]--></button>).</p><p>The cellular composition of brain organoids matches that of human brain tissue, consisting of neurons, glia, and microglia. The structure of organoids is similar to some regions of the human brain, making them ideal for studying intercellular interactions between neurons and glia (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B28-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b28"><!--[--><span id="B28a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Hong et al., 2022<!--]--></button>).</p><p>Organoids contain intermediate progenitor cells and neuronal cells at various stages of differentiation (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B70-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b70"><!--[--><span id="B70a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Salmina et al., 2021<!--]--></button>). Astrocytes (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B95-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b95"><span id="B95a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span>Yakoub, 2019</button>) and microglia (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B60-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b60"><!--[--><span id="B60a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Ormel et al., 2018<!--]--></button>) can spontaneously arise in organoids. Using proteomic and functional analysis, Dezonne et al. demonstrated that astrocytes isolated from organoids have similar functional pathways to those in the human cerebral cortex (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B18-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b18"><!--[--><span id="B18a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Dezonne et al., 2017<!--]--></button>). Dendritic spines and neural networks were also observed to appear in the organoids when cultured for more than 9 months (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B68-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b68"><!--[--><span id="B68a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Quadrato et al., 2017<!--]--></button>).</p><p>The structure of cerebral organoids is still imperfect for several reasons: due to the lack of a developed vascular system, the central part of the organoid becomes necrotic (<a href="#F1" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-f1"><!--[--><!--]--><!--[-->Figure 1<!--]--></a>), and even after long-term culture for 2 months, cerebral organoids are comparable only to the intrauterine level of brain development (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B47-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b47"><!--[--><span id="B47a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Logan et al., 2020<!--]--></button>). It has been established that cells in a 3D structure have a number of differences from cells forming a monolayer: shape, proliferation rate, pharmacological resistance, degree of differentiation, and gene expression (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B82-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b82"><!--[--><span id="B82a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Sogomonyan et al., 2022<!--]--></button>).</p><div class="ArticleFigure" id="F1"><div class="ArticleFigure__header"><p class="ArticleFigure__title">FIGURE 1</p><button data-tootip-position="top center" class="ButtonIcon ButtonIcon--black ButtonIcon--medium" aria-label="Download Figure" data-event="articleFigure-button-download"><!--[--><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 48 48" fill="currentColor" width="24px" style="vertical-align:middle;display:inline-block;height:auto;" color="black"><path d="M39.182 24.218 24 39.405 8.808 24.203a.96.96 0 0 1 0-1.358l1.358-1.354a.96.96 0 0 1 1.354 0l10.56 10.56V1.965a.96.96 0 0 1 .96-.96h1.91a.96.96 0 0 1 .96.96v30.1l10.56-10.56a.962.962 0 0 1 1.359 0l1.353 1.354a.96.96 0 0 1 0 style="vertical-align:middle;display:inline-block;height:auto;" color="black"><path d="M28 10V6h14v14h-4v-7.171L27.414 23.413l-2.828-2.828L35.172 10H28ZM6 28h4v7.172l10.586-10.586 2.828 2.828L12.828 38H20v4H6V28Z"></path></svg><!--]--></button></div><button class="ArticleFigure__figureButton" aria-label="Open lightbox for FIGURE 1"><figure class="FrontiersImage"><picture class="FrontiersImage"><!--[--><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=340&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g001.webp" media="(max-width: 563px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=612&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g001.webp" media="(max-width: 768px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=654&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g001.webp" media="(max-width: 1280px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=631&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g001.webp" media="(max-width: 1441px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=716&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g001.webp" media=""><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g001.webp" media=""><!--]--><img src="https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g001.webp" class="is-inside-mask" alt="Diagram of a spheroid and organoid cross-section showing three zones: proliferating cells on the outside, quiescent cells in the middle, and a necrotic core in the center. Arrows indicate nutrients, oxygen, and growth factors moving inward, and metabolic waste, carbon dioxide, and lactate moving outward." loading="lazy"></picture><figcaption><!--[--><!--[--><p>Average structure of spheroids and organoids. The main spatial zones observed during 3D cultivation are shown. Proliferating cells are the most metabolically active part of the cells; they have the greatest access to dissolved oxygen, nutrients, and signaling factors. This part of the cells enables the growth (and, under certain conditions, migration) of organoids and spheroids. Quiescent cells are resting cells; their level of metabolism, proliferation, and oxygen consumption is reduced due to reduced access to oxygen and nutrients. Upon exfoliation of the upper cell layers or migration to the matrix, these cells are able to re-increase their metabolic rate. Necrotic core is the inner part of spheroids and organoids (provided that they are not hollow). This part of the 3D structure contains virtually no living, metabolically active cells; most of the contents of this zone are cellular debris following necrosis or apoptosis.</p><!--]--><!--]--></figcaption></figure></button></div><p>Many research groups are already developing and exploring approaches to promote the maturation of organoid cells and interactions between them. One such approach is the generation of assembloids. Assembloids are formed by integrating multiple organoids (multiregional structures) or by fusing organoids with additional cell types (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B36-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b36"><!--[--><span id="B36a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Kanton and Paşca, 2022<!--]--></button>). For example, Fligor et al. created an assembloid model of the visual system incorporating cortical and thalamic organoids. Visual projection was modeled through the response of retinal glial cells to environmental cues. Furthermore, the survival of retinal cells in these assembloids was increased compared to retinal organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B23-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b23"><!--[--><span id="B23a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Fligor et al., 2021<!--]--></button>).</p><p>Upon fusion of medial ganglionic eminence and cortex-specific organoids, migration of ventral interneurons from the ganglionic eminence organoid into the cortex organoid was observed. This study provided the opportunity to analyze interneuron migration and integration in real time for further modeling and study of interneurogenesis in brain organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B94-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b94"><span id="B94a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span>Xiang et al., 2017</button>). Another approach to functional maturation of organoids is their transplantation into the animal brain. Mansour et al. implanted cerebral organoids cultured for 40–50 days into the retrosplenial cortex of immunodeficient mice. Organoid engraftment was confirmed to occur within 2–12 weeks, with neuronal cell maturation, gliogenesis, microglial integration, and organoid vascularization observed (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B49-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b49"><!--[--><span id="B49a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Mansour et al., 2018<!--]--></button>).</p><p>Ham et al. successfully addressed the issue of cerebral organoid vascularization by inducing the differentiation of vascular endothelial cells within them under the influence of VEGF. This was confirmed by the positive expression of the endothelial cell marker CD31 and the tight junction marker characteristic of the BBB, claudin-5 (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B26-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b26"><!--[--><span id="B26a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Ham et al., 2020<!--]--></button>). The next equally successful method for forming a vascular system in organoids was the co-cultivation of ESCs or iPSCs with human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B79-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b79"><!--[--><span id="B79a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Shi et al., 2020<!--]--></button>). It was noted that a high concentration of VEGF stimulates the differentiation of endothelial and neuronal cells in cerebral organoids. The formation of vascular tubes with a bilayer structure is facilitated by culturing cerebral organoids with VEGF and Wnt7a (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B26-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b26"><!--[--><span id="B26a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Ham et al., 2020<!--]--></button>). Another approach to organoid vascularization is the fusion of separately grown neural and vascular organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B93-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b93"><span id="B93a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span>Wörsdörfer et al., 2019</button>). As a result, fusion in cerebral organoids resulted in the formation of a vascular structure with an increased number of neural progenitors, which may support the hypothesis that neurogenesis is regulated by vessels. These hybrid organoids contained microglia, and direct contacts between endothelial cells and glial cells, which are important <!--[--><i>in vivo</i><!--]--> for the formation of the blood-brain barrier, were observed. The researchers note that such organoids may serve as a model for neurovascular interactions (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B85-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b85"><!--[--><span id="B85a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Sun et al., 2022<!--]--></button>). Ahn et al. also reported the formation of a vascular network within a cortical organoid as a result of its fusion with vascular cells (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B1-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b1"><!--[--><span id="B1a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Ahn et al., 2021<!--]--></button>). In addition to these methods, microfluidic technologies exist for the vascularization of cerebral organoids, for example, using 3D-printed microfluidic chips (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B71-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b71"><!--[--><span id="B71a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Salmon et al., 2022<!--]--></button>).</p><p>Thus, organoids already allow researchers to study complex biological processes to varying degrees in controlled conditions, overcoming the limitations of traditional two-dimensional (2D) cell cultures, which are unable to fully reproduce the complexity of cellular interactions and tissue architecture <!--[--><i>in vivo</i><!--]--> (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B48-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b48"><!--[--><span id="B48a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Lukonin et al., 2020<!--]--></button>).</p><!--]--></div><div id="s3"><!--[--><h2>3 The importance of organoid imaging</h2><p>Organoids, as promising tissue-engineering tools, require suitable imaging methods (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B57-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b57"><!--[--><span id="B57a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Morris and Payne, 2019<!--]--></button>) and accurate quantitative morphometric analysis. Such methods allow for the objective assessment of key parameters such as growth, differentiation, responses to various external stimuli (e.g., drugs), and the development of pathological changes (<a href="#F2" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-f2"><!--[--><!--]--><!--[-->Figure 2<!--]--></a>).</p><div class="ArticleFigure" id="F2"><div class="ArticleFigure__header"><p <!--[--><b>(A)</b><!--]--> Shape and size <!--[--><b>(B)</b><!--]--> Proliferating cells <!--[--><b>(C)</b><!--]--> Dead/dying cells <!--[--><b>(D)</b><!--]--> Cell types.</p><!--]--><!--]--></figcaption></figure></button></div><p>Many programs for morphometric analysis are also capable of detecting not only the presence of the features listed in points B, C, and D, but also quantifying them and accounting for their spatial distribution.</p><!--]--></div><div id="s4"><!--[--><h2>4 The importance of morphological analysis of organoids</h2><p>One of the key indicators of a mature, functionally healthy organoid is its morphology. This important parameter allows for the assessment of organoid quality (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B34-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b34"><!--[--><span id="B34a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Kahveci et al., 2025<!--]--></button>). For example, changes in organoid morphology at early stages and the formation of budding structures serve as key indicators of successful brain organoid differentiation. To optimize morphological analysis, image-capture microscopy methods and various image analysis software are used. Morphometric analysis is necessary not only for cell counting and type identification but also for research purposes. The importance of morphometric analysis of organoids is clearly illustrated in <a href="#F3" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-f3"><!--[--><!--]--><!--[-->Figure 3<!--]--></a>.</p><div class="ArticleFigure" id="F3"><div class="ArticleFigure__header"><p class="ArticleFigure__title">FIGURE 3</p><button data-tootip-position="top center" class="ButtonIcon srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=340&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g003.webp" media="(max-width: 563px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=612&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g003.webp" media="(max-width: 768px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=654&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g003.webp" media="(max-width: 1280px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=631&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g003.webp" media="(max-width: 1441px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=716&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g003.webp" media=""><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g003.webp" media=""><!--]--><img src="https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g003.webp" class="is-inside-mask" alt="Flowchart illustrating the importance of morphometric analysis of organoids, which includes evaluation for experiments, counting organoids, characterization of cell types and cellular components, pathological marker detection, cell migration study, and morphological parameter acquisition." loading="lazy"></picture><figcaption><!--[--><!--[--><p>Applications of organoid morphometric analysis: cell counting, characterization of cell types (stem and proliferating cells, neuronal populations, oligodendrocytes, astrocytes, microglia, epithelium) and cellular components (nuclei, mitochondria, synapses, dendrites, or axons); search for pathological markers, study of cell migration, investigation of tissue permeability and manifestations of necrosis; obtaining morphometric parameters of organoids and comparison of organoid groups with each other.</p><!--]--><!--]--></figcaption></figure></button></div><!--]--></div><div id="s5"><!--[--><h2>5 Spheroids and organoids imaging methods</h2><!--[--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><h3>5.1 Culture viability assessment</h3><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>To analyze cells in spheroids, some of the techniques used for analyzing conventional cell cultures can be used (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B16-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b16"><!--[--><span id="B16a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Danilova et al., 2021<!--]--></button>). In general, viability assessment methods can be divided into those that analyze the medium for the presence of cell death markers or dissolved colored compounds, and those that analyze the properties and staining of the entire spheroid. For example, the former include the LDH test (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B15-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b15"><!--[--><span id="B15a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Cox et al., 2021<!--]--></button>), the resazurin test, assessment of the leakage of fluorescent proteins from dead cells (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B46-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b46"><!--[--><span id="B46a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Lifanov et al., 2025<!--]--></button>), and assessment of the leakage of fluorescent dyes. The latter group includes a modification of the MTT or WST8 test followed by an assessment of the staining intensity of the entire spheroid <!--[--><i>in situ</i><!--]--> (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B67-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b67"><!--[--><span id="B67a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Popova et al., 2022<!--]--></button>) without solubilizing the colored or fluorescent reaction products; evaluation of fluorescence in the green and red channels using dyes that penetrate and do not penetrate living cells (e.g., calcein AM/propidium iodide) (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B73-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b73"><!--[--><span id="B73a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Saydé et al., 2024<!--]--></button>); measurement of total bioluminescence using appropriate reporter systems. W. Senkowski et al. utilized a rather mechanistic yet robust approach, using GFP under the CMV promoter as a non-specific indicator of the metabolic status of cells (specifically, protein synthesis processes), and assessed the cytotoxic effect of the tested promising anticancer agents based on the level of total spheroid fluorescence. Interestingly, in the same work, it was once again shown that some promising cytostatic drugs, which showed good activity against cancer cells in a 2D monolayer, did not exhibit their effect when moving to a 3D culture (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B77-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b77"><!--[--><span id="B77a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Senkowski et al., 2015<!--]--></button>).</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>Camarero et al. demonstrated the effect of laser radiation with wavelengths of 808 and 1,450 nm (near-infrared) on spheroids formed by epithelial cells of adenocarcinoma (an example of a tightly formed spheroid) and glioblastoma (an example of a spheroid with a large intercellular volume). Mature spheroids formed after laser irradiation changed in size, which correlated with spheroid viability. This allowed them to determine the irradiation thresholds for spheroid survival in laser microscopy experiments (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B12-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b12"><!--[--><span id="B12a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Camarero et al., 2023<!--]--></button>). Thus, changes in the size of 3D objects can serve as an indirect indicator of the viability of their constituent cells.</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--]--><!--[--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><h3>5.2 Pre-treatment of organoids for imaging</h3><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>Three-dimensional brain cultures, including cerebral organoids, are studied both whole-mount and at the molecular and cellular levels. Most often, microscopic examination is used to study cerebral organoids, followed by subsequent analysis using a variety of methods, most commonly computer processing. Cerebral organoids are first studied whole, in cleared form, and then in sliced form (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B10-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b10"><!--[--><span id="B10a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Brémond et al., 2021<!--]--></button>).</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><!--[--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><h4>5.2.1 Organoids preparation for study as a whole sample</h4><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>Optical clearing methods are used to study three-dimensional whole specimens, as it is important to reduce light scattering to ensure the refractive indices of various cellular components are consistent when imaging large three-dimensional structures (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B17-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b17"><!--[--><span id="B17a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Dekkers et al., 2019<!--]--></button>). Optical clearing allows the specimen to be made transparent using various solvents (organic solvents, highly refractive aqueous solutions, tissue hyperhydrating and transforming solutions). These protocols were developed for studying brain structures, but have also been applied to other organs and are used to study organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B51-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b51"><!--[--><span id="B51a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Matryba et al., 2019<!--]--></button>). Protocols also exist for clearing organoids while preserving their physiological functions and viability—for example, the isotonic clearing medium SeeDB-Live, described by Inagaki S. et al. (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B31-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b31"><!--[--><span id="B31a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Inagaki et al., 2024<!--]--></button>). A mixture of fructose and glycerol was used to clear live organoids from the respiratory tract, colon, kidney, liver, and mammary gland of humans and mice. The authors of the article note that this clearing protocol is applicable to other types of organoids as well (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B17-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b17"><!--[--><span id="B17a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Dekkers et al., 2019<!--]--></button>).</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--]--><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><!--[--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><h4>5.2.2 Organoids sections preparation</h4><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>Microscopic examination of three-dimensional brain structures requires preliminary preparation: fixation in paraformaldehyde to stop enzymatic reactions and cutting three-dimensional structures into sections of 5–50 μm thickness to facilitate visualization of cerebral organoids and spheroids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B49-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b49"><!--[--><span id="B49a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Mansour et al., 2018<!--]--></button>).</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--]--><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--]--><!--[--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><h3>5.3 Microscopic methods for studying cerebral organoids</h3><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>Most researchers use brightfield, light sheet, and confocal microscopy to address the diverse needs of cerebral organoid analysis (observation, morphometric analysis, and quantification) (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B10-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b10"><!--[--><span id="B10a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Brémond et al., 2021<!--]--></button>). Less than 2% of studies utilize inverted and phase-contrast microscopes (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B88-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b88"><!--[--><span id="B88a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Thorn, 2016<!--]--></button>). The principles of image formation in different types of microscopy are shown in <a href="#F4" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-f4"><!--[--><!--]--><!--[-->Figure 4<!--]--></a>.</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><div class="ArticleFigure" id="F4"><div class="ArticleFigure__header"><p class="ArticleFigure__title">FIGURE 4</p><button data-tootip-position="top center" class="ArticleFigure__figureButton" aria-label="Open lightbox for FIGURE 4"><figure class="FrontiersImage"><picture class="FrontiersImage"><!--[--><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=340&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g004.webp" media="(max-width: 563px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=612&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g004.webp" media="(max-width: 768px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=654&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g004.webp" media="(max-width: 1280px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=631&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g004.webp" media="(max-width: 1441px)"><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/api/ipx/w=716&f=webp/https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g004.webp" media=""><source srcset="https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g004.webp" media=""><!--]--><img src="https://www.frontiersin.org/files/Articles/1861934/xml-images/fcell-14-1861934-g004.webp" class="is-inside-mask" alt="Three labeled panels (A, B, C) illustrate different light microscopy setups using colored arrows and cones for illumination and detection relative to a cross-sectional diagram of tissue. Blue arrows labeled “Illumination” indicate light direction, orange arrows labeled “Detection” show detection direction, and a black horizontal line denotes the focal plane in each setup." loading="lazy"></picture><figcaption><!--[--><!--[--><p>The most common types of microscopy and a schematic representation of their principles. <!--[--><b>(A)</b><!--]--> Light microscopy–the most common type of microscopy. It can use either white light or light with a narrow spectral range (for fluorescence microscopy). <!--[--><b>(B)</b><!--]--> Confocal microscopy–most often used in fluorescence microscopy. Unlike light microscopy, images outside the focal plane are not captured by the detector (they do not pass through the pinhole). Often, when examining 3D objects, it is necessary to either physically section the spheroid or organoid being examined, or clear it with special solutions (see <a href="#s5-2-1" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-s521"><!--[--><!--]--><!--[-->Section 5.2.1<!--]--></a>. Organoids preparation for study as a whole sample). <!--[--><b>(C)</b><!--]--> Light sheet microscopy–a type of microscopy in which illumination occurs from the side and the light beam is shaped like a “sheet”. It is also typically used in conjunction with fluorescent staining. Unlike confocal microscopy, there is no need for a pinhole, and light sheet microscopy makes it easier to reconstruct an object in 3D. </p><!--]--><!--]--></figcaption></figure></button></div><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><!--[--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><h4>5.3.1 Confocal laser microscopy</h4><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>A confocal microscope is used more often than other fluorescence microscopes for visualizing organoids, as confocal microscopy allows for the study of organoids in 3D space, unlike light microscopy. However, unlike brightfield microscopy, which can preserve the original color of the specimens being studied, confocal microscopy requires the use of fluorescent labels (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B56-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b56"><!--[--><span id="B56a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Monzel et al., 2017<!--]--></button>). Confocal microscopy can also be used to obtain images without slicing organoids: for example, a method has been developed that allows for growing organoids up to 100 μm thick (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B19-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b19"><!--[--><span id="B19a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Durens et al., 2020<!--]--></button>).</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>Three-dimensional visualization offers the most effective means for obtaining shape information and quantifying three-dimensional cellular structures compared to other methods. Härmä et al. obtained confocal images of three-dimensional organotypic cultures of 12 prostate and breast cancer cell lines using a confocal microscope. These images were then processed using the AMIDA program described below. Using these combined methods, three drugs were identified that inhibit metastasis development without inhibiting proliferation and apoptosis in non-metastatic tissue (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B27-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b27"><!--[--><span id="B27a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Härmä et al., 2014<!--]--></button>).</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--]--><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><!--[--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><h4>5.3.2 Bright field microscopy</h4><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>In a study by Tomas et al., brightfield microscopy using the Incucyte® S3 organoid module, immunohistochemical staining, and immunofluorescence were used to compare the morphology of epithelial ovarian cancer spheroids and organoids (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B90-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b90"><span id="B90a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span>Tomas et al., 2025</button>). These methods revealed that organoids, unlike spheroids, were morphologically heterogeneous and differed in growth dynamics and proliferative activity (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B90-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b90"><span id="B90a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span>Tomas et al., 2025</button>).</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>Organoids grown using hydrogels cannot be aligned in the same focal plane, resulting in images in transmitted light with uneven blurring even at low magnification and low aperture values. Furthermore, the hydrogel medium contributes to distortion of phase and differential contrast images due to spherical aberration and brightfield noise (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B9-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b9"><!--[--><span id="B9a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Borten et al., 2018<!--]--></button>). These problematic aspects were addressed using the OrganoSeg automated system, which is capable of processing such images. This method is described below.</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><p>Thus, confocal laser microscopy is optimal for obtaining images of 3D objects, but requires the use of fluorescent labels. At the same time, brightfield microscopy is a more versatile tool for working with organoids, allowing not only to obtain organoid images for subsequent analysis but also to conduct observations at different stages of organoid development without special preliminary sample preparation. Below, we review programs for processing images obtained primarily using brightfield microscopy (OrganoSeg, AnaSP, BraIn, OrganoLabeler, OrganoID, Label Free Tracker, Trackmate, Elephant) and confocal laser microscopy (Fiji with ImgLib, Organoid Tracker, Trackmate). Some of the programs can process both types of images (Ilastik, AMIDA, MOrgAna). Some of these programs can also process organoid images obtained using phase-contrast microscopy (OrganoSeg, OrganoID). Light sheet fluorescence microscopy (LSFM) is also used to image 3D structures, but it is only used in 3% of organoid observation studies due to the high cost of an LSF microscope and sample preparation requirements (<button type="button" color="Blue40" id="B10-button" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-b10"><!--[--><span id="B10a" class="ArticleReference__scrollTarget" aria-hidden="true"></span><!--]--><!--[-->Brémond et al., 2021<!--]--></button>). Therefore, it is not considered in this review. <a href="#T1" class="ArticleReference" data-event="articleReference-a-t1"><!--[--><!--]--><!--[-->Table 1<!--]--></a> presents the microscopy methods and associated image processing software.</p><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><div class="ArticleTable" id="T1"><div class="ArticleTable__header"><p class="ArticleTable__title">TABLE 1</p><!----><button align="left">1 + 2+0 + 1 (4)</td><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--></tr><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><tr><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><td align="left">CellProfiler</td><!--]--><!--[--><!--]--><!--[--><td
